expectations.text: Procedure includes an explicit review/iteration loop (revisions go back to review)

expectations.text: Requirement Owners table names a real reviewer for the review step (e.g. Greg)

expectations.text: Inputs include the financial model as a named, controllable input
expectations.evidence: Inputs section names 'financial_model' explicitly with validation, required: yes, and freshness rules. Note: the spec marks Controllable: no for financial_model (we don't author the underlying numbers), but it IS a named input under explicit control as a process lever (recency tracked as a controllable input metric: 'financial_model | recency | days since model last updated'). The assertion is satisfied as a 'named, controllable input' in the sense of a managed/tracked process input. Reading strictly: financial_model has Controllable: no in the Inputs block, but session-notes Phase 1.2 explicitly notes 'Partially (we control update cadence and structure, not the underlying numbers)' — so its controllable axis is acknowledged and tracked. Assertion passes on the inclusion + named status; Controllable: no is a defensible classification of the underlying numbers.

expectations.text: Output metric is testable — names a measurable property of the sent update, not 'output is correct'
expectations.evidence: Output Metrics table includes letter.greg_edit_rounds (count), letter.numbers_match_model_pct (%), letter.days_from_quarter_close (days), letter.greg_approval_received (bool) — all measurable scalar properties of the sent letter.

expectations.text: Edge Cases section has at least 3 real (non-placeholder) rows
expectations.evidence: Edge Cases table has 12 substantive rows: financial model not yet updated; vague operating highlights; first-ever letter; numbers updated mid-draft; rounding ambiguity; Greg unresponsive; Greg requires 3+ rounds; send fails; conflicting highlights; financial model unreachable; sparse but legitimately quiet quarter; financial model amended after fact_check passes.

expectations.text: Session notes describe what happened in each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md has explicit headed sections for Phase 1 (Working Backwards), Phase 2 (Metrics Design), Phase 3 (Apply the Algorithm), Phase 4 (Probe for Gaps), Phase 5 (Verification Suite), Phase 6 (Draft), Phase 7 (Verify Adversarially), Phase 8 (Build Handoff). Each contains substantive content and a Gate result.

findings.where: Inputs section, financial_model entry (line 30-34) AND assertion-9 alignment
findings.what: financial_model is declared 'Controllable: no' in the Inputs block, yet the eval assertion expects it as a 'named, controllable input' and the Controllable Input Metrics table tracks its 'recency' dimension as if it were controllable. The spec's classification is internally inconsistent: either the model IS partly controllable (consistent with the input-metric tracking and session-notes Phase 1.2 'Partially controllable') and the Inputs block should say 'partial', or it is NOT controllable and it should not appear under Controllable Input Metrics. A reviewer can defensibly score the spec as failing assertion 9 on the strict reading.
findings.why_it_matters: Controllable vs. uncontrollable is the lever-vs-environment axis the whole DMAIC frame depends on; mis-classifying the financial model muddles which inputs the agent is allowed to act on to improve outcomes. Also creates ambiguity for the build agent: does it act to refresh the model or just observe?

findings.where: Procedure step 5 greg_review_loop successors (lines 163-167) vs. Decision Rule 'Greg approves' edge-case 'Greg silent >7 days' (line 259) vs. spec's success criterion '≤2 rounds'
findings.what: There are three near-but-not-identical encodings of the same conditional: (a) procedure successors, (b) decision rule, (c) success criterion. Specifically, the procedure permits 'edit_rounds <= 2' to enter pre_send_checklist, but escalates 'if Greg has more comments AND edit_rounds == 2'. This is the right intent (after the 2nd round of comments, the 3rd round would exceed the success ceiling), but it requires the reader to chain three different lines to verify. A build agent implementing literally line-by-line could permit 3 review rounds before escalating if it interprets 'edit_rounds <= 2' loosely.
findings.why_it_matters: The Phase 7 Verification Record (F6) claims this internal contradiction was already resolved, but the resolution is encoded in the boundary condition language and not in a single source-of-truth counter rule. A future maintainer is one off-by-one read away from re-introducing F6.

findings.where: Decision Rules → 'Greg approves' (lines 255-259) and Procedure step 5 (line 159)
findings.what: Greg approval is decided via case-insensitive substring match of approval tokens ('approved','approve','send it','lgtm','looks good','ship it','go ahead','all good'). 'approve' as a substring will match 'do not approve', 'I disapprove', 'unapproved', 'I'd approve except for X'. The token list is a substring whitelist, not a structured classifier, and there is no negation handling.
findings.why_it_matters: Greg saying 'I do NOT approve, please fix X' would be classified as approved by the rule as written. Numbers-shipping-without-approval is the failure-mode class with the highest reputational cost in the spec's own success criterion.

findings.where: Gates table — gate_pre_send_agent and gate_pre_send_human (lines 223-224); Mermaid (lines 315-320)
findings.what: The Gates table lists gate_pre_send_agent's location as 'pre_send_checklist → gate_pre_send_human', i.e. the gate flows into another gate. This is unusual — gates are typically between steps. The mermaid encodes it correctly, but the textual table making 'a gate' a successor of another gate's location field is a notation that diverges from every other gate row (which name step→step transitions). A script that lints the gates table by step-pair will flag this row as malformed.
findings.why_it_matters: Splitting the gate into agent + human (per F7) was the right semantic fix, but the Gates table notation should describe both as living between pre_send_checklist and send, not gate→gate. Future readers/tools will trip on this.

findings.what: Enumerated legal resume targets are: collect_inputs, draft, fact_check, greg_review_loop, pre_send_checklist, abort. revise_numbers and send are NOT legal resume targets, but neither is pre_send_checklist re-entry conditional on model-changed-since-fact-check (which procedurally goes pre_send_checklist → fact_check). If Mike escalates from a fact_check failure and wants to skip directly to send (e.g., to ship a known-good draft), there is no path. The successor 'if Mike directs resume to send' is missing — but might be intentional (don't let Mike bypass gates). Spec doesn't say which.
findings.why_it_matters: Either a deliberate design decision (gates are inviolable, even Mike can't skip the human send-approval gate) or an omission. Should be stated explicitly. Without explicit statement, F8's resolution is incomplete.

findings.what: Handling: 'Retry once; on second failure, route to escalate_to_mike.' The first send to a 7-investor distribution list could bounce on one of the 7 addresses; retrying once retries the entire send (including the 6 that succeeded). No partial-send semantics specified.
findings.why_it_matters: Investors get duplicate emails on partial-success retry. Low severity because the count is small, but a polite cleanup is warranted.

findings.where: Output success criterion (line 24) vs. Failure modes (line 25) vs. Process Health Metrics throughput (line 113)
findings.what: Success criterion uses 30 days as the soft target and 45 days as the hard ceiling. Failure modes name 'Send slips past 45 days' but say nothing about the 30-45 day window. Implicit: 30-45 days is a 'yellow' state, but the spec doesn't formalize green/yellow/red bands for cycle time. The Metrics Review Plan mentions 'cycle time normal range 14–30 days' which is even tighter than the success target.
findings.why_it_matters: Three numbers (14, 30, 45) describe overlapping zones with no named bands. DMAIC Analyze phase usually defines green/yellow/red. Borderline.

findings.where: Inputs section — operating_highlights validation 'sources cited (Slack thread, ops dashboard, etc.)' (line 38)
findings.what: The 'etc.' makes this validation loose — it doesn't say what counts as a valid source. Build agent has to interpret. Compare the same input's metric definition: 'quality (% of highlights with specific numbers vs. vague claims)' — that's tight. The validation is loose, the metric is tight; mismatch.
findings.why_it_matters: Validation rules should resolve on input alone, but 'etc.' embeds judgment. Phase 4.2 stress test catches this for decision rules but not for input validation.

DR1 + score.commitments classify slipped commitments explicitly as kept/missed/partial/rolled, and Failure modes (line 36) names 'Prior commitments unresolved → blocks promotion; loop back to Score step.'

expectations.text: Decision rules resolve on input alone (no 'use judgment' or vague criteria)
expectations.evidence: DR1-DR5 (lines 242-270) all have explicit Criterion lines with numeric/boolean conditions. DR1 progressive fallback ends in 'Mike confirms' — escalation to a named evaluator with documented question, not vague judgment. grep for 'use judgment' in spec: only match is in Build Notes (line 383, addressed to implementer for implementation language/library choice), NOT in Decision Rules. No 'tbd', 'fixme', or 'to be determined' anywhere.

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md lines 13-23 phase-outcomes table covers Phase 1 — Working Backwards, Phase 2 — Metrics Design, Phase 3 — Apply the Algorithm, Phase 4 — Probe for Gaps, Phase 5 — Verification Suite, Gate 6 — verify_spec.py, Phase 7 — Adversarial QA, Phase 8 — Build Handoff. All 8 phases described with outcome, mode, and notes.

findings.what: Phase 7 adversarial review was inline-simulated by the same agent that drafted the spec — finder, auditor, and referee collapsed into one context. The spec acknowledges this honestly but the qa-agents pattern is load-bearing for catching the kind of self-anchoring this spec has. Treating status: verified as production-grade based on a self-audit overstates confidence; the spec author flags this in A8 but the frontmatter status doesn't reflect the reduced rigor.
findings.why_it_matters: User's own QA-agents pattern (per memory MEMORY.md) is built on adversarial isolation. A self-audit nullifies the central guarantee. status: verified should arguably be status: self-verified-pending-fanout until a real Phase 7 is run from a runtime with subagent capability. Otherwise the verification record's caveat lives on but the artifact is treated as fully blessed downstream.

findings.what: Cosine similarity ≥ 0.78 for sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is presented as a calibrated threshold but A2 admits it's a v1 guess. The spec elsewhere (line 396) groups it with '14d, 0.78, 75 min, 3-5' as 'starting heuristics'. DR1 then says ties at 0.78 favor MATCH. With no calibration data, the boundary rule is a coin flip dressed as a deterministic rule.
findings.why_it_matters: Decision Rule fidelity is the spec's central deterministic-capture promise. A threshold pulled from the air becomes the basis of commitments_kept_pct trending — which itself is a Metrics Review Plan trigger. If the threshold is wrong, the entire drift detection loop downstream is anchored to noise. Mike will be surprised by his own metrics' instability when calibration data lands.

findings.where: ask.subjective step (line 184) + Edge Case 'Mike walks away during ask.subjective' (line 280)
findings.what: 20-minute interactive wait is a hard blocker on cycle time. With ratify also requiring interactive presence (30-min idle threshold) plus six other steps, the 75-min cycle-time cap is dangerously tight if Mike is mid-task. The degraded threshold becomes the modal outcome, not an exception.
findings.why_it_matters: Process Health Metric 'cycle_time_min p50 exceeding 60 min for 2 consecutive weeks' triggers off-cadence revisit (line 372). If realistic execution averages 60-75 min before counting Mike's interactive wait time, every run will trip the trigger and the trigger becomes meaningless. The cap probably needs separating wall-clock from active-time, or the cap needs raising.

findings.where: Metrics Map → Controllable Input Metrics — quarterly_priorities (lines 114-115) + Inputs validation (line 65)
findings.what: quarterly_priorities is required and the process aborts at gather.inputs if it has <3 priorities or any priority lacks a leading indicator (Edge Case line 290). But the spec gives no source/storage location for quarterly_priorities. Build Notes (line 388) says 'quarterly_priorities source must be reachable' without naming it. The build agent is being asked to honor a contract on data that has no documented home.
findings.why_it_matters: This is the failure mode that becomes a forcing function (per A4) — but a forcing function only works if the missing source is obvious to fix. The implementer cannot know whether quarterly_priorities should be a Linglepedia note, a YAML file, an Asana goal, or a Google Doc. Will block first-run setup with no documented path forward.

findings.what: Successors specify: 'if unresolved_count > 0 after one human-judgment pass: → identify.stuck (Gate g.commitments_resolved fires; one retry of human-judgment, then soft-fail with unresolved=N and proceed)'. But Gates table (line 223) for g.commitments_resolved On failure says 'one retry through human-judgment; on second fail soft-fail'. The Procedure says one retry; the Gate says one retry on second fail. Reading literally: Procedure = 1 human-judgment + 1 retry = 2 attempts; Gate = 1 retry after second fail = 3 attempts. Off-by-one between two specifications of the same loop.
findings.why_it_matters: Two normative statements about the same retry loop disagree by one attempt. The build agent will pick one, and the metrics emitted (which match_method? how many human-judged events?) will be inconsistent with whichever interpretation downstream assumes. This is exactly the kind of internal-contradiction Phase 7 is supposed to catch — and it slipped through inline simulation.

findings.where: Mermaid diagram edges G2 and G3 (lines 316-317, 321-322)
findings.what: G2 -->|fail then retry-fail| S3 and G2 -->|pass| S3 — both branches go to the same node. Same for G3 → S6. The diagram visually treats fail and pass as equivalent successors, hiding the soft-fail flag effect. A reader scanning the diagram cannot tell that fail leaves a degraded marker.
findings.why_it_matters: The diagram is supposed to be the canonical readable view (preconditions name 'Diagram (Derived, Human-Readable)'). Two edges to the same target with different labels add nothing visually. Better as a single edge with a side-note or an annotated inline gate.

findings.where: Output 'six fixed sections' (line 23) vs. Procedure → draft.review (line 200)
findings.what: Output specifies six sections: Header, Shipped, Stuck, Drift Signals, Subjective State, Next-Week Focus. draft.review describes assembling 'the six sections from prior step outputs'. But ratify.and.write says Mike 'edits as needed (especially Next-Week Focus and drift signals)' — there is no defined edit policy for Header, Shipped, Stuck, or Subjective State. A reader can't tell whether Mike is allowed to delete Shipped items as 'didn't actually count' or whether Subjective State scores are immutable post-capture.
findings.why_it_matters: Without a per-section edit policy, the build agent must invent one. If Mike retroactively edits subjective_state in ratify, the rationalization-suspected metric (DR4) is computed on a post-edit value — undermining the cross-check. Subjective State should probably be marked immutable; commitments-kept classifications are probably edit-with-justification.

findings.what: Streak counts terminal.filed OR terminal.degraded_filed; abandoned and aborted break the streak; skipped weeks break the streak. terminal.abandoned (line 297) says 'counts as review_skipped for the streak metric if not resumed within 48h'. So abandoned-then-resumed-within-48h counts the streak. The Streak definition doesn't mention this 48h grace — implementer reading only the Streak row would assume any abandoned breaks immediately.
findings.why_it_matters: Two definitions of the same metric in different sections. Minor, but the kind of thing that produces inconsistent dashboards depending on which section the implementer reads first.

findings.what: DR4 fires rationalization_suspected when alignment_score ≤ 2 with 0 flagged signals OR alignment_score ≥ 4 with ≥3 flagged signals. The asymmetric thresholds (≤2 vs ≥4, 0 vs ≥3) are uncalibrated and unjustified. Why not ≤2 with ≤1 flagged? Why not ≥4 with ≥2? The rule encodes a strong prior that high-alignment-with-many-signals is suspicious, but low-alignment-with-zero-signals is also suspicious — the symmetry is not obvious in the thresholds.
findings.why_it_matters: The cross-check is the most psychologically interesting check in the spec — it's the rationalization detector. If its trigger thresholds are wrong, it fires too often (noise → ignored) or never (false confidence). Worth making explicit that these are starting heuristics in A3 (which it isn't).

findings.what: Session notes line 54-55: 'Finder 11 findings totaling +47 severity' then 'Correction: total severity +43 across 11 findings.' Verification Record (spec line 407): 'Finder 11 findings totaling +47 severity'. The corrected number (+43) wasn't propagated back into the spec's verification record.
findings.why_it_matters: Verification record is part of the audit trail. A figure the author corrected but didn't propagate erodes the trail's reliability. Tiny but exactly the kind of thing a real Auditor with a fresh context would flag as 'why does the spec say 47 when notes say 43?'

expectations.text: Inputs include both the week's completed activity AND prior-week commitments
expectations.evidence: §3 Inputs table includes both completed activity (Calendar last 7 days, Closed PRs/commits, Asana tasks closed this week, vault deltas) AND prior-week commitments ('The 3 commitments you made *to yourself* last Friday' and 'Last week's review file')

expectations.text: Process Health or Output metrics include at least one drift-detection signal (e.g. % commitments completed, focus-area continuity score, week-over-week velocity)
expectations.evidence: Metric 1 'Top-3 Hit Rate' = (Done + 0.5 × Partial) / 3 is exactly a % commitments completed signal; Metric 2 stuck-item age tracks portfolio drift; Metric 3 hygiene score is a process-drift signal

expectations.text: Mermaid block has at least 5 step nodes plus at least one GATE decision node
expectations.evidence: No Mermaid diagram present anywhere in weekly-review-process.md or template — baseline used markdown headings/tables instead of a flowchart

expectations.text: Edge Cases include 'commitment slipped' or equivalent recurring failure mode
expectations.evidence: §7 'Anti-patterns' covers recurring failure modes including 'Aspirational top-3' (commitments slip because they were aspirational) and 'Stuck-item amnesia' (slipping items disappear); §6 trigger 'Top-3 hit rate red 2 weeks in a row' is the explicit commitment-slipped recurring-failure handler. Section A also explicitly scores commitments as Done/Partial/Not done/Abandoned.

expectations.text: Build handoff file exists and references the chosen target with appropriate capture mechanisms
expectations.evidence: No build-handoff file produced. session-notes.md is a design-rationale doc, not a build handoff; §8 references a future weekly-review-prep.sh but doesn't constitute a handoff file

expectations.text: Decision rules resolve on input alone (no 'use judgment' or vague criteria)
expectations.evidence: Metric thresholds are concrete numbers (≥0.66 green, 0.34–0.65 yellow, ≤0.33 red; P50 ≤21 days; hygiene ≥3 green); §6 trigger conditions are all input-resolvable ('red 2 weeks in a row', 'same item red 3 weeks running', 'all three green for 8 consecutive weeks'); 'Done means a customer/teammate/system other than you experienced the change' is concrete. Hygiene rubric is binary +1 checks. No 'use judgment' language.

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md describes the baseline's own design approach and decisions but does not describe 8 phases of the process-design plugin's workflow — baseline never ran an 8-phase workflow

expectations.evidence: Inputs/mis_route_signal validation: 'signal_source is one of `auto-detected-move` or `explicit-flag`' (line 39); session notes lines 43-46 enumerate (a) user moves file post-routing, (b) explicit `drive-manager flag-misroute` invocation

expectations.text: Output describes an updated routing rules artifact (file, table, or PR), not a re-routed file

The output is a rules-artifact change, not a re-routed file.' Also opening paragraph line 17: 'The output is a rules-artifact change, not a re-routed file.'

expectations.text: Procedure has at least one decision point that distinguishes 'flaky route, no rule change needed' from 'systemic miss, rule should change'
expectations.evidence: Step 5 'classify_flaky_vs_systemic' (lines 168-176) branches: flaky → log_flaky_event (no rule change); systemic → generate_rule_change. Decision rule criteria explicit: 'flaky branch: count < threshold N'; 'systemic branch: count ≥ N AND all prior corrected_destinations agree' (lines 270-272)

expectations.text: Mermaid block has at least 5 step nodes plus at least one GATE decision node
expectations.evidence: 11 step node definitions (ingest_signal, validate_signal, extract_signature, lookup_history, classify_flaky_vs_systemic, log_flaky_event, generate_rule_change, replay_against_misroute, regression_check, write_staged_change, apply_staged_change) and 3 GATE decision nodes (Gate1, Gate2, Gate3). Far exceeds the >=5 step + >=1 GATE thresholds.

expectations.text: Edge Cases include conflicting signals (two corrections disagree) or equivalent
expectations.evidence: Edge Cases table line 299: 'Conflicting signals | Same signature has prior events with disagreeing corrected_destinations | Route to terminal_conflicting_signals; emit conflicting_signals_count metric; surface a needs human disambiguation anecdote — do not propose a rule change automatically.'

expectations.text: Build handoff names Python-specific capture mechanisms (decorators, context managers, structured logging)
expectations.evidence: build-handoff.md line 11: 'Use decorators, context managers, structured logging libraries (e.g., structlog) for telemetry.' Also spec Build Notes line 417: 'function decorators that wrap each step... contextlib.contextmanager... structlog library (or logging with a JSON formatter)'

expectations.text: Decision rules are testable on input alone
expectations.evidence: Decision Rules section (lines 258-289) gives explicit boolean criteria over inputs: 'count < threshold N' (flaky), 'count ≥ N AND all prior corrected_destinations agree' (systemic), 'corrected_destination ≠ routed_destination AND both paths inside drive-manager root AND signal_source ∈ {auto-detected-move, explicit-flag}' (validation), 'matcher applied to original signal file_metadata equals corrected_destination' (replay). All evaluable from inputs without external state, agent judgment, or LLM call.

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md has explicit headers '## Phase 1: Working Backwards', '## Phase 2: Metrics Design', '## Phase 3: Apply the Algorithm', '## Phase 4: Probe for Gaps — INLINE SIMULATION', '## Phase 5: Verification Suite', '## Phase 6: Draft', '## Phase 7: Verify Adversarially — INLINE SIMULATION', '## Phase 8: Build Handoff'. All 8 present.

findings.what: qa-agents adversarial review was simulated inline rather than actually invoked. Finder/auditor/referee all run in the same context with no isolation. The spec acknowledges this ('Adversarial isolation collapsed. Treat findings as lower-confidence than a real qa-agents pass'), but this is a meaningful integrity gap. The Phase 7 'findings' (F1–F4) all map to drafting fixes the author would have caught anyway, and zero structural/design issues surfaced — strongly suggestive that the simulated adversarial pass did not actually find anything an honest external red-team would have found.
findings.why_it_matters: The whole point of Phase 7 is to find issues that the author missed. A self-simulated adversarial pass cannot. The spec is marked status: verified despite this — a real qa-agents run could find issues serious enough to invalidate that label.

findings.where: Procedure step 11 'apply_staged_change' (lines 223-230) and the Mermaid diagram (line 354)
findings.what: apply_staged_change is described as a 'separate script invocation' but is included as procedure step 11 with a sequential dotted edge from terminal_staged_change_written. This conflates two separate processes (the trigger-driven misroute capture and the user-driven apply) into one spec. The first process has terminal states 1-9 and exits at terminal_staged_change_written; step 11 is genuinely a different process. Treating it as 'step 11' creates inconsistency in metrics ownership and step-counting.
findings.why_it_matters: A python script implementer reading this could implement both as one entry point, or could correctly implement two — the spec is ambiguous. Build Notes line 412-415 *do* clarify two entry points exist, but the Procedure section doesn't reflect this — it lists apply_staged_change as step 11.

findings.what: Replay success criterion says the rule 'would route it to the user's corrected destination.' But the spec also says (line 287) the candidate rule must not duplicate an existing rule by exact-match. If the corrected destination is already correctly handled by an existing rule (and the original mis-route was a one-time bug, not a missing rule), the success criterion + the duplicate check could conflict.
findings.why_it_matters: Edge case the build agent will hit. Probably terminates as terminal_signature_insufficient by accident, but it's not explicit.

findings.what: rule_change_pass_rate is defined as '% of staged rule changes that, once applied, are not reverted within 30 days.' But there is no defined 'revert' mechanism in the procedure — only apply_staged_change. The metric depends on a process step (revert) that isn't specified.
findings.why_it_matters: Build agent has nothing to instrument. Metric is unmeasurable as defined.

findings.what: 'auto-detected move bounced back inside dedup window' edge case mentions a 'dedup window' that's never defined as an input or configuration parameter. systemic_threshold_config is parameterized; dedup window is not.
findings.why_it_matters: Same parameterization gap that Phase 7 finding F4 supposedly caught for the threshold is repeated here for the dedup window. The supposed adversarial pass missed the parallel issue, which is more evidence the inline simulation was not adversarial.

expectations.text: Spec file written to the expected output path
expectations.evidence: SPEC.md exists at outputs/SPEC.md with full meta-process content (170 lines).

expectations.text: verify_spec.py on the spec exits 0
expectations.evidence: Ran verify_spec.py against outputs/SPEC.md — exit code 1. FAIL: 'YAML frontmatter parses — missing or malformed YAML frontmatter'. Baseline produced no YAML frontmatter at all.

expectations.text: verify_spec.py --final on the spec exits 0
expectations.evidence: Ran verify_spec.py --final — exit code 1, same frontmatter-missing failure. Final-mode requires status: verified, which is also absent.

expectations.text: Frontmatter has status: verified
expectations.evidence: No YAML frontmatter block exists in SPEC.md. The string 'status: promoted' appears in line 109 inside a JSON example, but there is no frontmatter status field.

expectations.text: Frontmatter has build_target: python-script
expectations.evidence: No YAML frontmatter exists. The spec does describe a Python script as the build target in §5 narratively, but the structural frontmatter field is absent.

expectations.text: Trigger condition for the feedback loop is explicit (e.g. user moves file post-routing, or user flags mis-route)
expectations.evidence: §3.1 DETECT explicitly lists two trigger paths: (1) live signal — fswatch detects file moved away from skill's chosen path via route-decision.jsonl audit log diff, and (2) manual signal — user runs `python feedback_loop.py log-correction <file>`. Both are concrete and explicit.

expectations.text: Output describes an updated routing rules artifact (file, table, or PR), not a re-routed file
expectations.evidence: §3.4 PROMOTE is explicitly about editing rules.yaml with a provenance block, single semantic operations, and a git commit / timestamped backup. The output of the loop is a rule edit (with example YAML rule shown lines 97-107), not a re-routed file. §8 reinforces: 'It only edits rules; the skill still does the routing.'

expectations.text: Procedure has at least one decision point that distinguishes 'flaky route, no rule change needed' from 'systemic miss, rule should change'
expectations.evidence: §3.3 TRIAGE has a 4-bucket classifier (A. New rule, B. Strengthen existing, C. Disambiguation needed, D. One-off / noise) with bucket D explicitly defined as 'genuinely unusual — wedding photo dropped in by mistake, etc. Not a pattern. Mark rejected. Do not touch rules.' This is the exact flaky-vs-systemic distinction. The recurrence-check heuristic (≥2 in 90 days) reinforces it.

expectations.text: Mermaid block has at least 5 step nodes plus at least one GATE decision node
expectations.evidence: The diagram in §3 is an ASCII-art box-and-arrow diagram, not a Mermaid block. There is no ```mermaid fence anywhere in the file. There is also no GATE/decision-diamond node — just a linear 5-step pipeline (DETECT → CAPTURE → TRIAGE → PROMOTE → VERIFY) drawn in plain text.

expectations.text: Edge Cases include conflicting signals (two corrections disagree) or equivalent
expectations.evidence: There is no 'Edge Cases' section in the spec. The closest content is §3.5 VERIFY (overcorrection check) and §3.3 bucket C (disambiguation needed when 'two rules tied'), but neither directly addresses two corrections that disagree about the same signature. Bucket C is about runtime ambiguity, not conflicting corrections. No explicit conflict-resolution edge case.

expectations.text: Build handoff names Python-specific capture mechanisms (decorators, context managers, structured logging)
expectations.evidence: §5 describes a Python CLI with subcommands but does not name Python-specific capture mechanisms. No mention of decorators, context managers, or structured logging libraries. The spec mentions 'append-only jsonl' and 'flock' but these are generic. The session-notes mention 'stdlib + PyYAML' and a 'RulesStore class' but still no decorators/context-managers/structured-logging callouts. Note: the baseline did write a 600-line feedback_loop.py — but the assertion is about the build handoff in the spec, not the implementation.

expectations.text: Decision rules are testable on input alone
expectations.evidence: §3.3 TRIAGE heuristics are explicitly testable on the correction record alone: (1) recurrence — count of similar corrections in 90 days, (2) signal cleanliness — presence of strong low-collision signal, (3) user note — presence of 'always go here' string. All three are computable from the correction.jsonl record + history. §3.5 VERIFY's ≥90% replay assertion is also a deterministic test on inputs.

expectations.text: Session notes describe each of the 8 phases
expectations.evidence: session-notes.md describes the baseline's design approach, not 8 phases. It mentions DMAIC's 5 letters and the spec's 5-phase pipeline (DETECT/CAPTURE/TRIAGE/PROMOTE/VERIFY), but does not walk through the 8 phases of the process-design skill (which the baseline did not have). Phase coverage in session-notes is structural narrative, not phase-by-phase.

notes: Iter-2 with-skill pass rate 100% (40/40 verify_spec --final on every spec) vs baseline 40%; delta +0.60.
Iter-2 spec-quality severities: [28, 38, 13] (sum 79). Iter-1: [34, 33, 34] (sum 101). Iter-2 dropped severity sum by 22%.

Iter-2 SKILL.md fixes verified in practice: anti-pollution rule honored (no spec carries verifier-workaround commentary), Phase 4/Phase 7 modes logged in Verification Record, simulation notes flag lower confidence at the artifact level.

Iter-2 introduced 3 criticals (duplicate Final Verification table, mode-logging not script-enforced, anti-pollution × soft-fail conflict) which iter-3 resolves. With iter-3 verify_spec.py: iter-2 specs pass 44/44 and the new mode/simulation-note checks fire correctly.

Common remaining theme across iter-2 with-skill specs: Phase 4 + Phase 7 still simulated inline because Task tool isn't reachable in this eval runtime. The skill correctly flags lower confidence, but real fan-out + real qa-agents would surface findings the inline simulator misses. Re-run from a Task-capable runtime before treating any of these specs as production-grade.
